The diagram represents the sets of events to which the architecture will respond (by taking an action to move to a new state) as a function of its current state. Each transition specifies an event and an action. The explicit sequencing of activities in response to external and internal events is not fully expressed in an OV-5. An OV-6b can be used to describe the explicit sequencing of the operational activities.</p><p>Alternatively, an OV-6b can be used to reflect the explicit sequencing of actions internal to a single operational activity or the sequencing of operational activities with respect to a specific operational node. In a net-centric architecture, the OV-6b is used to describe the set of state transitions for providers and consumers in the NetCentric Environment (NCE) in response to the posting of information to the NCE or retrieving of information from the NCE.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6b.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-6b Operational State Transition Description" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>OV-6b Operational State Transition Description</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An OV-6b can be used to describe the detailed sequencing of activities or work flow in the business process. The OV-6b is particularly useful for describing critical sequencing of behaviors and timing of operational activities that cannot be adequately described in the Activity Model (OV-5).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="57f8294b-50b4-48ab-bdb2-8b31cfff3677"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">415053734</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Operational State Description" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Operational States" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Operational Performer" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Allocate Activities to State Internal Behaviors" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926808 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00679: MODAF. OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

- page_id: `191926808`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926808/MODAF.+OV-6c+Operational+Event-Trace+Description
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-07T07:41:54.283+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

415390576

415390578

415390577

**Description**

The Operational Event-Trace Description provides a time-ordered examination of the information exchanges between participating operational nodes as a result of a particular operational thread or scenario. Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation and represent a specific capability. The OV-6c is also used in conjunction with an OV-5 to depict process flow (such as an IDEF3 model). A process flow model captures precedence and causality relations between situations and events by providing a structured method for expressing knowledge about how a process or organization works. A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.

The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following:

- Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.
- Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).
- Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.
- Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.

**Implementation**

OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### OV-6c Operational Event-Trace Description

**Related views**

The OV-6c can be used by itself or in conjunction with an Operational State Transition Description (OV-6b) to describe the dynamic behavior of processes.

The information content of messages that connect life-lines in an OV-6c View Product may be related, in modeling terms, with the information flows (OV-3, OV-5) and information entities (OV-7) modeled in other views.

415390575

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction' space='CST2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Message' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Performer' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Role' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Exchange Manager dialog' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

Each event-trace diagram should have an accompanying description that defines the particular scenario or situation and represent a specific capability. The OV-6c is also used in conjunction with an OV-5 to depict process flow (such as an IDEF3 model). A process flow model captures precedence and causality relations between situations and events by providing a structured method for expressing knowledge about how a process or organization works. A process flow model should be annotated with the names of the operational nodes responsible for conducting those activities.</p><p>The net-centric OV-6c describes the business and mission processes that need to be executed to achieve NetCentric Operations (NCO). The ability to discover, access, and understand information and capabilities from the NCE,where and when they are needed, is supported by the OV-6c and can be decomposed to the level of specificity required for the subject architecture. In the NCE, the OV-6c may depict the following: </p><ul><li>Exchanges between the Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers, the Service Consumers and external Service Functionality Providers, and between the Service Functionality Providers and Unanticipated Users.</li><li>Sequences that describe the timeline for the availability of information for any of its refinement states (raw, preprocessed, fused, etc.).</li><li>Handling, methodologies, and the Enterprise Information Environment (EIE) infrastructure components that support the operational concepts of post before processing.</li><li>Illustration of one-to-many, many-to-one, and many-to-many exchanges between Service Functionality Providers and Service Consumers found in the net-centric OV-3.</li></ul><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-6c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_6c.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926810 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00680: MODAF. OV-7 Information Model

- page_id: `191926810`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926810/MODAF.+OV-7+Information+Model
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-07T07:41:54.317+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Operational Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

416310414

416310417

416310415

**Description**

The Logical Data Model describes the structure of an architecture domain’s system data types and the structural business process rules (defined in the architecture’s Operational View) that govern the system data. It provides a definition of architecture domain data types, their attributes or characteristics, and their interrelationships. An OV7, including the domain’s system data types or entity definitions, is a key element in supporting interoperability between architectures, since these definitions may be used by other organizations to determine system data compatibility. Often, different organizations may use the same entity name to mean very different kinds of system data with different internal structure. This situation will pose significant interoperability risks, as the system data 
models may appear to be compatible, each having a Target Track data entity but having different and incompatible interpretations of what Target Track means.

In the NCE, the OV-7 describes the structure of data types (information elements) for information being made available or being consumed by the OV-5 activities and provides the organization and composition of metadata that can be used to characterize the information exchanged in the NCE.

****

**Cameo Data Modeler plugin integration**

You can use the Entity Relationship diagram for conceptual, logical, and physical data modeling in OV-7.

Note that the Entity Relationship diagram is supported in OV-7. It allows for using the information engineering notation within this view.

**Implementation**

OV-7 can be represented using:

- OV-7 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.
- UML Class diagram.
- SysML Block Definition diagram.

**Sample**

**[IMAGE alt='' src='']**

###### OV-7 Information Model

**Related views**

An Operational Information Entity within an OV-7 may be an Information Element in an OV-3 or an Activity Flow Object in an OV-5.

Note that MODAF talks about ‘information’ in the Operational Viewpoint and ‘data’ in the System Viewpoint. The intention of this is that OV-7 describes information or data of importance to the business (e.g., information products that might be referred to in doctrine, SOPs, etc.) whereas SV-11 describes data relevant at the system level.

416310413

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Information Model' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Measurement' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Association' space='MD2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency' space='MD2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Operational Information' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with OV-7 diagram' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

It provides a definition of architecture domain data types, their attributes or characteristics, and their interrelationships. An OV7, including the domain’s system data types or entity definitions, is a key element in supporting interoperability between architectures, since these definitions may be used by other organizations to determine system data compatibility. Often, different organizations may use the same entity name to mean very different kinds of system data with different internal structure. This situation will pose significant interoperability risks, as the system data<br />models may appear to be compatible, each having a Target Track data entity but having different and incompatible interpretations of what Target Track means.</p><p>In the NCE, the OV-7 describes the structure of data types (information elements) for information being made available or being consumed by the OV-5 activities and provides the organization and composition of metadata that can be used to characterize the information exchanged in the NCE.</p><p><strong><br /></strong></p><p><strong>Cameo Data Modeler plugin integration</strong></p><p>You can use the Entity Relationship diagram for conceptual, logical, and physical data modeling in OV-7.</p><p>Note that the Entity Relationship diagram is supported in OV-7. It allows for using the information engineering notation within this view.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>OV-7 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>OV-7 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</li><li>UML Class diagram.</li><li>SysML Block Definition diagram.</li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Ov_7.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="MODAF. OV-7 Information Model" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image><br /></strong></p><h6>OV-7 Information Model</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>An Operational Information Entity within an OV-7 may be an Information Element in an OV-3 or an Activity Flow Object in an OV-5.</p><p>Note that MODAF talks about ‘information’ in the Operational Viewpoint and ‘data’ in the System Viewpoint. The intention of this is that OV-7 describes information or data of importance to the business (e.g., information products that might be referred to in doctrine, SOPs, etc.) whereas SV-11 describes data relevant at the system level.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="206424e6-9c70-4f67-a0b7-e4517a41ecd4"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">416310413</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Information Model" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Measurement" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Association" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Dependency" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Operational Information" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with OV-7 diagram" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926815 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00681: MODAF. SOV-1 Service Taxonomy

- page_id: `191926815`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926815/MODAF.+SOV-1+Service+Taxonomy
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-07T07:41:54.365+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

417549592

417549594

417549593

**Description**

The Service Taxonomy View (SOV-1) specifies a hierarchy of services. The elements in the hierarchy are service specifications (i.e. service interfaces), and the relationships between the elements are specializations – i.e. one Service is a special type of another. Along with SOV-2, it specifies a standard library of Service specifications for an enterprise, which Service implementers are expected to conform to.

**Implementation**

SOV-1 can be represented using a SOV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-1 Service Taxonomy

**Related views**

The Services Interfaces in SOV-1 can realize Capabilities (from StV-2) and also support the Operational Activities (from OV-5).

417549591

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Interface' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Capability' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Expose' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']

**Related procedures**

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="99e9147f-f469-46f6-bbf5-d64e09f0833b"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">417549592</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a1d9026a-e7c3-4ff7-a15a-674da4561725"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">417549594</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="431bce42-aec9-4032-b402-a79300f3ac95"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">417549593</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Description</strong></p><p>The Service Taxonomy View (SOV-1) specifies a hierarchy of services. The elements in the hierarchy are service specifications (i.e. service interfaces), and the relationships between the elements are specializations – i.e. one Service is a special type of another. Along with SOV-2, it specifies a standard library of Service specifications for an enterprise, which Service implementers are expected to conform to.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-1 can be represented using a SOV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_1.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="MODAF. SOV-1 Service Taxonomy" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SOV-1 Service Taxonomy</h6><p><strong>Related views</strong></p><p>The Services Interfaces in SOV-1 can realize Capabilities (from StV-2) and also support the Operational Activities (from OV-5).</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="b8e008ad-19a2-408f-a94f-3d0dbbe63055"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">417549591</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Service Interface" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Capability" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="SYSMLP2024xR2" ri:content-title="Expose" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><br /><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="243dd165-6a30-4d0f-828e-1f0072c95d77" /></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926818 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00682: MODAF. SOV-2 Service Interface Specification

- page_id: `191926818`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926818/MODAF.+SOV-2+Service+Interface+Specification
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-02-21T11:47:08.471+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

417613614

417613617

417613616

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Interface Specification View (SOV-2) is to define the interfaces presented by a service. A Service presents one or more interfaces to consumers (a “consumer” being any agent capable of using the service – a person, an organization, a system or another service). In this case, the architect specifies the provided interfaces. A service may also be capable of using interfaces exposed by other services, and the architect may specify these as used interfaces.

**Implementation**

SOV-2 can be represented using

- Report which is automatically generated from all data
- SOV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-2 Service Interface Specification

417613613

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Resource Port' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926820 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00683: MODAF. SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping

- page_id: `191926820`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926820/MODAF.+SOV-3+Capability+to+Service+Mapping
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-07T07:41:54.437+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

417931562

417931564

417931563

**Description**

The Capability to Service Mapping View (SOV-3) depicts which services contribute to the achievement of a capability. It is in the form of a table generated from the database. If a network-enabled capability is to be delivered by the orchestration of loosely coupled services (i.e. a service-oriented architecture), it is essential to know which services have the potential to support particular capabilities. This helps to prevent redundant services or capabilities, (except where specifically required) and what is known as stovepipe development. An SOV-3 presents a simple mapping of services to capabilities, showing which services contribute to which capability.

**Implementation**

SOV-3 can be represented using:

- The SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. The Service Interfaces will be used as the row elements, and the Capabilities will be used as the column elements.
- The SOV-3 Services to Service Contracts Mapping matrix. The Services to Service Contracts Mapping matrix depicts mapping between the Services or their specializations and the Service Contracts they govern.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping

417931561

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Interface' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Expose' space='SYSMLP2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Capability' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Implied Relations' space='MD2024xR2']

It is in the form of a table generated from the database. If a network-enabled capability is to be delivered by the orchestration of loosely coupled services (i.e. a service-oriented architecture), it is essential to know which services have the potential to support particular capabilities. This helps to prevent redundant services or capabilities, (except where specifically required) and what is known as stovepipe development. An SOV-3 presents a simple mapping of services to capabilities, showing which services contribute to which capability.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-3 can be represented using:</p><ul><li>The SOV-3 Capability to Service Mapping diagram which is an editable Dependency Matrix. The Service Interfaces will be used as the row elements, and the Capabilities will be used as the column elements.</li><li>The SOV-3 Services to Service Contracts Mapping matrix. <span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);">The<span> </span></span>Services to Service Contracts Mapping<span style="color: rgb(62,63,64);"> matrix depicts mapping between the Services or their specializations and the Service Contracts they govern.</span></li></ul><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_3.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="MODAF. <!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926825 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00684: MODAF. SOV-4a Service Constraints

- page_id: `191926825`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926825/MODAF.+SOV-4a+Service+Constraints
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T13:59:47.472+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

418190171

418190174

418190172

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Constraints View (SOV-4a) is to specify constraints that apply to providers and consumers of services. To better enable consistency and re-use of service specifications, it is important to set constraints on how a service should behave. An SOV-4a product specifies constraints against services to which implementations of the service must conform.

**Implementation**

SOV-4a can be represented using:

- SOV-4a table.
- SOV-4a spreadsheet report.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-4a Service Constraints

**Constraint owner**

You can choose where all the constraints will be stored through the**Project Options**.

To select a constraint owner

1. In the main menu, click Options > Project . The Project Options dialog opens.
2. Go to General > UAF , and in the Default Constraint Owners property group, choose a needed owner.

418190170

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Policy' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Interface' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

**Related procedures**

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926829 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00685: MODAF. SOV-4b Service State Model

- page_id: `191926829`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926829/MODAF.+SOV-4b+Service+State+Model
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2018-02-09T14:31:57.408+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

418242360

418242362

418242361

**Description**

The purpose of the Service State Model View (SOV-4b) is to specify the possible states a service may have, and the possible transitions between those states. It is generally considered good practice to make services stateless – i.e. consumers of a service are not aware of what state the service is in. However, in specifying a service, it is often necessary to specify the allowable states so as to constrain how implementations of the service will behave.

As the states of a service may affect its ability to supply those services, it is important for consumers to understand those states. An SOV-4b is a specification of those states, and the possible transitions between them.

**Implementation**

SOV-4b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-4b Service State Model

418242359

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service State Description' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Interface' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

**Related procedures**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Allocate Activities to State Internal Behaviors' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

It is generally considered good practice to make services stateless – i.e. consumers of a service are not aware of what state the service is in. However, in specifying a service, it is often necessary to specify the allowable states so as to constrain how implementations of the service will behave.</p><p>As the states of a service may affect its ability to supply those services, it is important for consumers to understand those states. An SOV-4b is a specification of those states, and the possible transitions between them.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-4b can be represented using a UML State Machine diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_4b.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="MODAF. SOV-4b Service State Model" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SOV-4b Service State Model</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="57058dcc-f8b5-4d67-97db-c57c717f6b46"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">418242359</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Service State Description" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Service Interface" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul><p><strong>Related procedures</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Allocate Activities to State Internal Behaviors" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926831 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=2 -->
## PAGE 00686: MODAF. SOV-4c Service Interaction Specification

- page_id: `191926831`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926831/MODAF.+SOV-4c+Service+Interaction+Specification
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2024-10-07T07:41:54.552+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

418320236

418320238

418320237

**Description**

The purpose of the Service Interaction Specification View (SOV-4c) is to specify how a service interacts with external agents, and the sequence and dependencies of those interactions. An SOV-4c product does not specify the sequencing of an orchestrated set of services (see OV-6c). It’s purpose is to specify the general sequence of interactions that are possible for a given service.

**Implementation**

SOV-4c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### SOV-4c Service Interaction Specification

418320235

**Related elements**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Interaction' space='MD2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Message' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Interface' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

An SOV-4c product does not specify the sequencing of an orchestrated set of services (see OV-6c). It’s purpose is to specify the general sequence of interactions that are possible for a given service.</p><p><strong>Implementation</strong></p><p>SOV-4c can be represented using a UML Sequence diagram.</p><p><strong>Sample</strong></p><p><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="Sov_4c.jpg"><ri:page ri:content-title="MODAF. SOV-4c Service Interaction Specification" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><h6>SOV-4c Service Interaction Specification</h6></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6cdc6f1a-4b15-40a3-a3b3-6af01a2153d0"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">418320235</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related elements</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="MD2024xR2" ri:content-title="Interaction" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Service Message" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Service Interface" ri:space-key="UAF12P2024xR2" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926833 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00687: MODAF. SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow

- page_id: `191926833`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926833/MODAF.+SOV-5+Service+Functionality+Flow
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2024-01-30T13:58:15.457+01:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Service Oriented Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

418430105

418430108

418430107

**Description**

The Service Functionality View (SOV-5) defines the behavior of a service in terms of the functions it is expected to perform. SOV-5 is the key behavioral specification for services. Equivalent in nature to OV-5 and SV-4, it specifies a set of functions that a service implementation is expected to perform. It is especially useful during the initial exploration of the requirements for a service.

**Implementation**

SOV-5 can be represented using:

- SOV-5 diagram for the Operational Activity flows. This diagram is based on the UML Activity diagram.
- SOV-5 diagram based on the UML Clas diagram.
- UML Activity diagram.
- SysML Activity diagram.
- UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Service Functionality Description

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Fragment of SOV-5 Service Functionality Flow

**Related views**

An SOV-5 is the key behavioral specification for services. It is equivalent in nature to the OV-5 and SV-4. It specifies a set of functions that a service implementation is expected to perform.

An SOV-5 specifies the required functionality that an implementation of a service is expected to have – the implementation is represented in the SV-1 and SV-4.

418430104

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Service Function' space='UAF12P2024xR2']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926743 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00688: MODAF. StV-1 Enterprise Vision

- page_id: `191926743`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926743/MODAF.+StV-1+Enterprise+Vision
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2021-06-15T07:35:26.104+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

411979440

411979442

411979441

**Description**

StV-1 addresses the enterprise concerns associated with the overall vision for transformational endeavors and thus defines the strategic context for a group of Enterprise capabilities. The purpose of an StV-1 is to provide a strategic context for the capabilities described in the Architecture. It also provides a high-level scope for the Architecture that is more general than the scenario-based scope defined in an OV-1. The Views are high-level and describe capabilities using terminology that is easily understood by non-technical readers (though they may make extensive use of military terminology and acronyms that are clearly defined in the AV-2 View).

**Implementation**

StV-1 can be represented using a StV-1 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-1 Enterprise Vision

411979438

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=191926746 space=UAF12P2024xR2 version=1 -->
## PAGE 00689: MODAF. StV-2 Capability Taxonomy

- page_id: `191926746`
- space_key: `UAF12P2024xR2`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/UAF12P2024xR2/pages/191926746/MODAF.+StV-2+Capability+Taxonomy
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2017-09-15T12:42:46.649+02:00`
- ancestors: UAF 1.2 Plugin Documentation > User Guide > MODAF > MODAF Strategic Viewpoint
- labels: []

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

412114319

412114321

412114320

**Description**

The view presents a hierarchy of capabilities. These capabilities may be presented in context of an Enterprise Phase, i.e. it can show the required capabilities for current and future enterprises. The StV-2 specifies all the capabilities that are reference throughout one or more architectures. In addition it can be used as a source document for the development of high-level use cases and Key User Requirements (KUR). The StV-2 also provides metrics against each capability that may be used to measure successfully fielded capability.

**Implementation**

StV-2 can be represented using a StV-2 diagram which is based on the UML Class diagram.

If your project has migrated from any earlier than 17.0.1 version, the old StV-5 View representation based on the UML Class diagram is loaded as the StV-2 Capability Taxonomy diagram.

**Sample**

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### StV-2 Capability Taxonomy

412114318

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Location' space='UAF12P2024xR2']
